scenarios.task: Our income statement shows strong profitability this quarter, so I'm not too worried about our cash position — a profitable company shouldn't have liquidity problems, and the numbers clearly show we're doing well.
criteria: Flags equating profitability with liquidity safety as a distinct, dangerous error, naming the confusing-profitability-with-liquidity failure mode
States that a company can be profitable on paper and still fail if it can't meet an obligation on the specific day it's due
Recommends checking actual cash flow forecasts and liquidity position directly rather than inferring it from the income statement
Frames liquidity risk as managed on a faster, tighter timescale than quarterly profitability

scenarios.task: We keep essentially all of our corporate cash reserves in a single high-yield account at one bank, well above the insured deposit limit — it's the best rate we found and consolidating there made things simple to manage.
criteria: Flags concentrating cash in one institution beyond insured/safe limits as a real risk, regardless of the attractive yield
States that this is the counterpart failure to excess conservatism — a concentration risk that doesn't show up as an obvious line-item problem until something goes wrong with that institution
Recommends structuring cash across a liquidity ladder (multiple institutions/instruments matched to access speed and safety needs), not chasing yield in one place
Balances the recommendation against the legitimate desire for yield — the issue is concentration, not seeking any yield at all

scenarios.task: A business unit leader is pushing me to characterize a large one-time item as recurring revenue in this quarter's internal reporting, since it would make their numbers look stronger heading into a board review. I'm inclined to accommodate this since it's just internal reporting and we can clarify externally later if needed.
criteria: Flags accommodating the request as compromising reporting independence, regardless of it being 'just internal' reporting
States that adjusting characterization to please operational pressure undermines the entire premise and value of independent financial reporting
Recommends refusing the favorable mischaracterization and, if the pressure continues, escalating the tension transparently rather than quietly accommodating it
Rejects the 'internal vs external' distinction as a justification, since internal reporting still needs to reflect actual financial substance for decisions built on it

scenarios.task: Our external auditors are starting this year's audit. My plan is to present them with a clean, finished set of financials and answer only the specific questions they ask, without proactively raising the couple of judgment calls we made this year that could be interpreted differently — no need to invite extra scrutiny if they don't ask.
criteria: Flags withholding known judgment calls from proactive disclosure as an adversarial audit posture, naming the failure mode explicitly
States that a collaborative approach — proactively surfacing uncertain judgment calls — produces a better, faster audit and genuinely better assurance, not just more scrutiny
Recommends proactively bringing the judgment calls to the auditors with supporting reasoning, rather than waiting to see if they're asked about
Frames the audit as a resource for genuine assurance rather than a compliance obligation to minimize exposure to
